title: Tigelle
subtitle: Small griddle-cooked bread rounds from Emilia-Romagna
excerpt: Tigelle are small, round, slightly yeasted flatbreads cooked in a cast-iron pan or griddle rather than the oven. They puff slightly as they cook, forming a pocket that you split open and fill with anything from cured meat and squacquerone cheese to Nutella. They are the Emilian street-food tradition and one of the simplest yeast doughs to make.
sourceNotes: Tigelle, also called crescentine in parts of Emilia-Romagna, are traditionally cooked between two heavy terracotta or stone plates (the tigella) over an open fire or gas flame. Modern home versions use a heavy frying pan or cast-iron griddle. The dough appears in Pellegrino Artusi, 'La Scienza in Cucina' (1891) under regional Emilian bread preparations. The combination of lard and milk distinguishes this dough from plain flatbreads.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool and freeze in bags. Reheat from frozen in a dry pan for 3 minutes per side.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made up to 24 hours ahead and kept covered in the fridge.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: cooked in a heavy pan on the hob over medium heat, no oven required

glossaryTerms.definition: Any bread cooked directly on a hot, dry, flat surface rather than in an oven. The heat source can be a cast-iron pan on a hob, a dedicated griddle plate, or a bakestone. Griddle breads include tigelle, English muffins, crumpets, Welsh cakes, and chapati. They cook faster than oven breads and need to be turned once, when bubbles appear on the surface.

content.text: and they cook entirely on the hob. The dough is quick to make (15 minutes by hand) and after a 60-minute rest it rolls and cooks in under 20 minutes. The lard gives a slight richness; olive oil can be substituted.

items.prepNote: at room temperature, or olive oil

content.text: Combine 300 g plain flour, 4.5 g yeast, and 4.5 g salt in a bowl. Rub in 25 g lard until crumbly. Add 120 ml warm milk and 60 ml tepid water and mix to a soft, smooth dough. Knead for 5 minutes. Cover and leave at room temperature for 60 minutes until doubled.

On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough to about 5 mm thick. Cut out rounds with a 7 to 8 cm cutter or the rim of a glass. Gather the trimmings, re-roll once, and cut again. You should have 12 rounds.

Heat a heavy cast-iron pan over medium heat; no oil, no butter. Cook the tigelle in batches of 3 or 4, 3 to 4 minutes per side, until both faces are deep gold with dark patches in places. The tigella should feel firm when pressed. Stack in a clean tea towel to keep warm while you cook the rest.
To serve, split each open with a knife and fill immediately while still warm: the pocket holds the heat and warms the filling.

items.problem: Tigelle are raw in the middle
items.solution: The pan was too hot and the outside browned before the inside cooked through. Reduce the heat to medium-low and cover the pan with a lid for the first 2 minutes of each side to trap heat.

items.problem: Dough does not puff into a pocket
items.solution: The tigelle needs to be thick enough — aim for 5 mm before cooking, which will puff to about 8 mm. Thinner discs will cook through without forming a pocket.
The pan was too hot. Cast iron holds heat well; reduce the flame before adding the next batch. Correct colouring is deep golden with a few darker spots, not black.
